content.body:
Wave equation: v = fλ
v = wave speed (m/s), f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m)
Period T = 1/f

Types:
Transverse: oscillation ⊥ to direction of travel (EM waves, water)
Longitudinal: oscillation ∥ to direction of travel (sound, P-seismic)

Wave properties: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference

content.title: Young's Double Slit Experiment
content.body:
Coherent light → two slits → interference pattern
Constructive (bright fringe): path difference = nλ
Destructive (dark fringe): path difference = (n+½)λ

Fringe spacing: w = λD/d
w=fringe width, D=distance to screen, d=slit separation

Proves light has wave nature

Diffraction:
Waves spread around obstacles/through gaps
Maximum diffraction when gap ≈ wavelength
Explains: radio waves bending around buildings,
sound diffracting around corners

questions.question: Wave speed v =
questions.explanation: Wave equation: v = fλ. Speed = frequency × wavelength.

questions.question: Frequency and period are related by
questions.explanation: Period T is the time for one complete cycle. Frequency f = cycles per second = 1/T.

questions.question: In Young's experiment, bright fringes form where path difference =
questions.explanation: Constructive interference (bright fringes): waves arrive in phase, path difference = whole number of wavelengths.

questions.question: Fringe spacing in Young's experiment: w = λD/d. Increasing slit separation d
questions.explanation: Fringes get closer when slits are further apart (d↑ → w↓). Wider slits = smaller fringes.

questions.question: Sound is a longitudinal wave because
options: particles oscillate parallel to wave direction
questions.answer: particles oscillate parallel to wave direction
questions.explanation: In longitudinal waves, particle displacement is parallel to wave propagation (compressions and rarefactions in sound).

questions.question: Diffraction is most significant when
questions.explanation: Maximum diffraction occurs when the gap or obstacle size is comparable to or smaller than the wavelength.

questions.question: Constructive interference occurs when
options: waves combine to give larger amplitude (in phase)
questions.answer: waves combine to give larger amplitude (in phase)
questions.explanation: Constructive interference: waves arrive in phase (crest meets crest) → amplitudes add → brighter/louder.

questions.question: Young's experiment proved light has wave nature because
options: it showed interference patterns (only waves interfere)
questions.answer: it showed interference patterns (only waves interfere)
questions.explanation: Interference is a uniquely wave phenomenon. Young's double slit fringes proved light behaves as a wave.

questions.question: Wavelength of sound with frequency 340Hz and speed 340 m/s
Polarisation is possible for
questions.explanation: Polarisation restricts oscillation to one plane — only possible for transverse waves (EM waves). Longitudinal waves cannot be polarised.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Lens formula:
1/f = 1/v + 1/u  (real-is-positive convention)
f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance

Magnification m = v/u = image height/object height

Power of lens P = 1/f  (dioptres, D, f in metres)
Positive f = converging lens; negative f = diverging lens

content.title: Types of Lenses
content.body:
Converging (convex) lens: brings parallel rays to focus
Object beyond 2f: real, inverted, diminished image (camera)
Object between f and 2f: real, inverted, magnified (projector)
Object inside f: virtual, upright, magnified (magnifying glass)

Diverging (concave) lens:
Always virtual, upright, diminished image

content.title: Eye Defects and Correction
content.body:
Myopia (short-sighted): eyeball too long, image forms before retina
Correction: diverging (concave) lens

Hyperopia (long-sighted): eyeball too short, image behind retina
Correction: converging (convex) lens

Astigmatism: uneven cornea curvature → blurred
Correction: cylindrical lens

questions.question: Lens formula is
questions.explanation: Lens formula (real-is-positive): 1/f = 1/v + 1/u

questions.question: Image formed by lens with f=10cm, object at u=30cm (v?)
Magnification =
questions.explanation: m = v/u (image distance / object distance). Also = image height / object height.

questions.question: Power of a lens with focal length 25cm (0.25m)
questions.explanation: P = 1/f = 1/0.25 = 4 dioptres

questions.question: A person is short-sighted (myopic). Correct with
questions.explanation: Myopia: light focuses in front of retina. Diverging lens spreads light slightly so it focuses on the retina.

questions.question: A magnifying glass uses a converging lens with object
questions.explanation: Object inside f → converging lens produces virtual, upright, magnified image on same side as object.

questions.question: A camera uses a converging lens with object
questions.explanation: Camera: object beyond 2f → real, inverted, diminished image on film/sensor.

questions.question: Total internal reflection is used in
options: optical fibres — light repeatedly reflects inside the fibre
questions.answer: optical fibres — light repeatedly reflects inside the fibre
questions.explanation: TIR keeps light inside optical fibres as it travels — requires angle greater than critical angle at each boundary.

questions.question: Which lens always produces a virtual image?
questions.explanation: Diverging (concave) lens always forms a virtual, upright, diminished image regardless of object position.

questions.question: The human eye focuses light using
options: variable-focus lens (changes shape by ciliary muscles)
questions.answer: variable-focus lens (changes shape by ciliary muscles)
questions.explanation: The crystalline lens changes shape (accommodation) by relaxing/contracting ciliary muscles to focus on near/far objects.

questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Optics Full concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Optics Full builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Optics Full. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Optics Full. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
